summary: I was born in Albemarle, North Carolina, in Stanley County on January 20, 1921. It’s where my family roots are, and I grew up nearby before going off to school. Albemarle is really the starting point of my life story.

Stanley County is where I was raised and where my family still lives. I went to school there, including a small country school in Endy. Both my younger brother and sister still live in Stanley County, so it remains very important to me.

I attended a little country school in Endy, North Carolina, and graduated high school there in 1939. It was a small class of twelve students, and I was the first boy from that school to ever go to college. Endy was really the foundation of my early education.

entity: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
summary: I started at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill in 1939, never having been there before. I had $112 saved from working in a cotton mill and was encouraged by Roy Armstrong, who was head of the pre-college guidance committee. While there, I worked for Bob Madry in the News Bureau, played freshman baseball under coach Jim Tatum, and eventually became editor of the student newspaper, competing with Jimmy Wallace. I was also honored to be tapped for the Golden Fleece and Order of the Grail societies during my time at Chapel Hill.

Roy Armstrong was the head of the pre-college guidance committee at UNC Chapel Hill when I arrived in 1939. I remember walking into his office with just $112 and asking if I could get through school on that amount. His encouragement—that many had done it with less—was the greatest support I ever received at that time.

Roy Armstrong later became head of the Morehead Scholarship program, which I knew about during my time at Chapel Hill. Although I didn’t receive the scholarship myself, Armstrong’s role in that program was notable and connected to my early university experience.

Bob Madry was the director of the University News Bureau at UNC Chapel Hill, and he gave me a job there as a freshman. I worked in the News Bureau all four years, and I often say I learned as much from Bob Madry as from my classes. He was a major influence in my early journalism career.

Jim Tatum was the coach of the freshman baseball team at UNC Chapel Hill when I was a student. I made the team as a catcher, though I was a sub and didn’t get to play much. It was a memorable part of my college experience, even if I was small and low on the depth chart.

Jimmy Wallace was my competitor for the editor position of the student newspaper at UNC Chapel Hill. I was eventually elected editor over him, and I understand he later became a professor at State College. We crossed paths during my university journalism days.

During my time at UNC Chapel Hill, I was honored to be tapped as a member of the Golden Fleece society. It was a prestigious recognition, and I remain very proud of that achievement. It reflected my involvement and standing among my peers at the university.

Alongside the Golden Fleece, I was also selected for the Order of the Grail at UNC Chapel Hill. Being part of both societies was quite an honor and a highlight of my college career. It showed my commitment and recognition in the university community.

The attack on Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941, happened right in the middle of my junior year at Chapel Hill. That event changed everything, leading me to work in a shipyard in Wilmington before enlisting in the Navy in 1942. It was a pivotal moment that shifted my life toward military service.

After Pearl Harbor, I worked in the shipyard in Wilmington, North Carolina, for seven months during the early war years. I was draftable and unmarried, so I decided to enlist in the Navy rather than continue building ships. Wilmington was my first wartime job location before active service.

I enlisted in the United States Navy in 1942 and served until 1946. I was Assistant Master-at-Arms at the hospital in Charleston, South Carolina, and later attended midshipman’s school at Chapel Hill and Notre Dame. I finished the war as an assistant navigator on the USS Xenobia, serving in the North Atlantic.

While in the Navy, I was stationed at the hospital in Charleston, South Carolina, where I served as Assistant Master-at-Arms. My commanding officer there recommended me for midshipman’s school, which I attended afterward. Charleston was an important stop in my naval career.

After my time at Chapel Hill, I attended one term at the University of Notre Dame as part of midshipman’s school during World War II. I earned a full year’s college credit from my combined studies at Chapel Hill and Notre Dame. This education helped me become an Ensign in the Navy.

I served as an assistant navigator on the USS Xenobia, an armed cargo assault ship, during the latter part of World War II. We operated in the North Atlantic, and this was my final naval assignment before discharge in 1946. It was a significant part of my military service.

I moved to Tabor City in 1946 after finishing my service and college requirements. The town was looking for someone to start a newspaper, and I took on that challenge with very little money and a young family. Tabor City became my home and the place where I built my journalism career.

The Tabor City Merchants' Association was instrumental in bringing me to town. They ran ads looking for someone to be their executive secretary and to start a newspaper. I applied for both, was hired as secretary, and then a few months later started the newspaper under their support.

My wife, Lucille Miller, was from Richfield, North Carolina, which is near my home county. We married before I left the service and moved together to Tabor City. Richfield is part of my family and personal background.

When I started the newspaper in Tabor City around 1948 or 1949, I first became aware of the Ku Klux Klan's attempt to revive activity in the area, including Columbus and Horry County. I wrote a weekly column opposing the Klan, calling it obsolete and unnecessary in a free society. This stance put me in direct conflict with Klan members and shaped much of my work in the following years.

Columbus, North Carolina, was one of the places where the Ku Klux Klan was trying to revive their presence in the late 1940s. I reported on and opposed their activities as part of my work with the Tabor City newspaper. Columbus was part of the regional context of the Klan’s resurgence.

Horry County, South Carolina, was another area where the Ku Klux Klan was active during the time I was running the newspaper in Tabor City. I became aware of their meetings and efforts there and spoke out against them publicly. Horry County’s Klan activity was part of the broader regional challenge we faced.
